coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis optimization of graphene quantum dots by Doehlert design for dye photodegradation application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Dutra, Luana Ventura
de Oliveira Fontoura, Camilla Rocha
da Cruz, Jean Castro
Nascimento, Mayra Aparecida
de Oliveira, André Fernando
Lopes, Renata Pereira
coredata.dc:description:
In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) of brewer’s spent grains. Doehlert matrix design was used to evaluate the effects of temperature and residence time on the GQDs’ properties, including their photocatalytic activity, totaling 9 experiments. Methyl orange (MO) was used as a model molecule for the photodegradation assays. The GQDs presented pH values smaller than 3.13, high electric conductivity (1.0–3.0 mS cm−1) and positive Zeta Potential values. The GQDs sample, when excited by UV light at 365 nm, emitted a blue color, confirming their self-doping with nitrogen. Hexagonal rings in zigzag edge structures were observed using Transmission Electron Microscopy. The GQDs presented quantum yields ranging from 0.3 % to 4.9 %. The GQDs produced at 150 °C for 14 h presented the best photocatalytic performance. The photodegradation of the MO occurred by a first-order mechanism, which comprises two stages. The stage 1 is attributed to the adsorption of MO by the GQDs and stage 2 is attributed to the degradation of the MO by
e
BC
−
and/or O2 - radicals. Finally, it can be concluded that GQDs with photocatalytic activity were produced by green synthesis, collaborating for a circular and sustainable dynamic in industrial processes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) of brewer’s spent grains. Doehlert matrix design was used to evaluate the effects of temperature and residence time on the GQDs’ properties, including their photocatalytic activity, totaling 9 experiments. Methyl orange (MO) was used as a model molecule for the photodegradation assays. The GQDs presented pH values smaller than 3.13, high electric conductivity (1.0–3.0 mS cm−1) and positive Zeta Potential values. The GQDs sample, when excited by UV light at 365 nm, emitted a blue color, confirming their self-doping with nitrogen. Hexagonal rings in zigzag edge structures were observed using Transmission Electron Microscopy. The GQDs presented quantum yields ranging from 0.3 % to 4.9 %. The GQDs produced at 150 °C for 14 h presented the best photocatalytic performance. The photodegradation of the MO occurred by a first-order mechanism, which comprises two stages. The stage 1 is attributed to the adsorption of MO by the GQDs and stage 2 is attributed to the degradation of the MO by
e
BC
−
and/or O2 - radicals. Finally, it can be concluded that GQDs with photocatalytic activity were produced by green synthesis, collaborating for a circular and sustainable dynamic in industrial processes.

ce:section.ce:para: The generation of solid wastes and their correct environmental management are issues caused by the modernity advance. Approximately 140 billion tons of biomass waste are generated annually in the world [1]. In Brazil, most of the biomass waste comes from the agricultural sector, especially the brewing industry. The brewing industry accounts for about 2 % of the Brazilian Gross Domestic Product (GDP), and this country is the third-largest producer in the world [2]. In the production process, brewer’s spent grains (BSG) is the main solid waste, corresponding to approximately 85 % of the total waste generated, i.e., about 1.7 million tons per year of bagasse [3,4].

BSG is commonly designated to animal feeding. However, its high humidity, high organic matter content, and management issues hinder this application. Therefore, it is necessary to develop nobler applications for the BSG, especially those that benefit from its high carbon content and heterogeneity. These applications would minimize environmental impacts and provide new alternative destinations for the residues generated. Therefore, the obtention of added-value products from BSGs via green synthesis has been studied recently, aiming for sustainability and low-cost process, promoting a zero-waste circular economy. Due to their surface properties, high conductivity, stability, malleable structure, and low toxicity, the generated carbonaceous materials can be used as adsorbents, in catalytic reactions, cell biology, and biomedicine, among others [5,6].

In 2004, the promising carbon dots (CDs) were discovered. Structurally, CDs can be classified as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and polymer dots (PDs) [7,8]. The GQDs have a size range of 2–20 nm and surfaces functionalized by different organic groups, such as hydroxyl, epoxy, and carboxylic acid. Furthermore, GQDs have sp2 hybridized carbons surrounded by crystalline domains, which form a 0.24 nm lattice spacing which can be attributed to the (1 1 2 0) diffraction planes of graphene [5,8–10]. The GQDs, as well as other carbon-based materials, were initially synthesized from non-renewable sources, such as mineral coal [11] and petroleum coke [12]. However, these sources are scarce. In this context, biomass waste emerges as an attractive alternative to produce GQDs, because it is renewable, presents high carbon content, and has lower cost, thus being environmentally friendly [5,13]. Using biomass waste for the GQDs synthesis makes it possible to transform a low-value material, or one that requires investment for proper handling and that causes adverse environmental impacts, into materials with appealing properties [6,14,15].

GQDs present photoluminescence, biocompatibility, chemical stability, photo-induced electron transfer, good solubility in water, and can be easily functionalized [14,15]. These properties enable their use in biomedicine [16], sensors [17], solar cells [18] and photocatalytic reactions [10,19]. The latter is because GQDs can absorb UV, visible and near-infrared photons, generating electron-hole (e-/h+) pairs. Additionally, GQDs are outstanding electron acceptors because of their surface defects that act as electron scavengers, delaying the recombination of the electron-hole pair [8,20]. The photocatalysis interest occurs because of its potential for organic pollutants degradation in aqueous systems [8,19]. Das et al. [20] produced CDs from pear juice via a green process and then applied them for the methylene blue dye degradation. Zhu et al. [21] synthesized CDs from palm residue powder by HTC using thionyl chloride as an activator. The produced material was applied in the photodegradation of dyes, such as rhodamine B and methylene blue [21]. Chen et al. [22] used the reactive red 2 as a precursor to synthesize CDs.

There are different methodologies for synthesizing GQDs. However, the hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) process has attracted substantial attention. This process comprises the thermochemical degradation of organic molecules or polymers in the presence of water, or an organic solvent, in a closed system at moderate temperatures (100–300 °C) and self-generated pressures [6,13,14,22–26]. The advantages of this process are its higher energy efficiency, low cost, simplicity, versatility, environmental friendliness, and results in particles with practically uniform sizes [6,26].

Experimental designs can facilitate the study of material properties affected by multiple factors because they enable important information gathering with the minimum number of experiments [27]. In this sense, it is interesting to investigate the GQDs' characteristics by varying two or more factors and analyzing their photocatalytic efficiency. Gomes et al. [28] performed an experimental factorial planning to determine the composition of CDs synthesized using different precursors, evaluating the band-gap energy and quantum yield as responses, aiming to apply these materials to solar energy conversion. The Doehlert design (DOE) has drawn attention among other response surface models because of its experimental practicality, economic savings, and the advantage of having different levels for each factor [29]. The DOE has a constant variance from the central point, allowing the possibility of investigating adjacent domains, adding new variables gradually, and requiring fewer experiments compared with other models [30].

Therefore, this work aimed to optimize the synthesis of GQDs by HTC using a DOE, having the temperature and residence time as the studied factors. The GQDs were synthesized using BSG as the precursor biomass. All the studied materials were characterized by physical-chemical analytical methods and were applied in experimental photodegradation assays of the methyl orange (MO) dye, used as a model molecule.

The following analytical grade reagents were used in the experiments: 85 % phosphoric acid (CAS 7664-38-2) and sodium acetate (CAS 127-09-3), purchased from Alphatec; fluorescein sodium (CAS 518-47-8), purchased from Synth; MO (CAS 547-58-0), purchased from Pro Analysis; ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid disodium salt and Na2-EDTA (CAS 6381-92-6), purchased from Dinâmica; silver nitrate (CAS 7761-88-8); purchased from Neon; t-butyl alcohol (CAS 75-65-0), purchased from Vetec; and p-benzoquinone (CAS 106-51-4).

A MO stock solution (1000 mg L−1) was prepared and used to prepare the working solutions. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water obtained from a Milli-Q system (Millipore Corporation) and stored under refrigeration at 9 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomass obtention and GQDs green synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The malt bagasse used in this study resulted from the production of a Pilsner type of beer (Agrarian) from Viçosa city, Minas Gerais, Brazil. Approximately 500 g of the material have been previously dried at 100 °C for 24 h and then grounded in a blender. The processed material was stored in amber glass bottles at room temperature until the further steps.

The HTC of the BSGs was conducted following a procedure adapted from the one reported by El Korhani et al. [31]. The reaction was performed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (80 mL). First, 6.25 g of the biomass waste was added to the autoclave, then 50 mL of a phosphoric acid solution (0.100 mol L−1), used as an activation agent, was also added [31]. The influence of the temperature and reaction time parameters was evaluated using a DOE with two variables and three experiments at the center point, as shown in Table 1.

Nine assays were performed. After the HTC process, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the products were centrifuged (FANEM, Excelsa Baby II, 206R) at 4000 rpm for 30 min and separated. The resulting colloidal suspension was filtered and stored under refrigeration at 9 °C for further analysis and application. The photocatalytic efficiency of the obtained suspensions was the evaluated response of the DOE. All experimental data were analyzed using the Statistica 7.0 software.

The pH values of the suspensions were measured using a LAB1000 bench pH meter working with an AF405 MS TECNOPON sealed glass electrode. The electrical conductivity (EC) values were measured using a Digimed DM32 conductivity meter. The redox potential values were measured at room temperature of 25 °C using a KASVI - K39-1014B instrument. The zeta potential values were determined using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS.

The functional groups of all obtained suspensions were analyzed by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) in a Bruker VERTEX 70 instrument, using the Attenuated Total Reflectance (ATR) method in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The morphology and size of the GQDs were analyzed by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) using a Tecnai G2-12 - Spirit Biotwin FEI - 120 kV microscope. The GQDs suspensions were analyzed using an UV-Vis Molecular Absorption Spectrophotometer (Thermo scientific - Evolution Array) equipped with a quartz cuvette (10.0 mm optical path), being the samples diluted (50 µL of the suspension in a 10.00 mL volumetric flask) before the readings.

The photoluminescence properties and quantum yield (QY) were analyzed in an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a 3.5 mL cuvette (10 mm optical path). The QY was calculated using the Eq. (1).

The GQDs’ photocatalytic efficiency was evaluated through the photodecomposition of the MO dye, used as a model molecule, under controlled temperature. The ultraviolet light irradiation (λ = 100–280 nm) was provided using four UV lamps (LUCMAT LAMPS, germicidal fluorescent lamp, 15 W GL) contained in a closed chamber equipped with two ventilation coolers (GC 8025, 12 V, 0.15 A).

20.00 mL of the GQDs suspensions, obtained from BSG according to experimental design described above (Table 1), were added to 20.00 mL of MO solution (100 mg L−1), resulting in a MO concentration of 50 mg L−1 [21,34]. A cylindrical reactor with a 7.4 cm diameter, wrapped with refrigerated hoses connected to a thermostatic bath, was used to keep the temperature constant at 25 °C. The UV lamps were turned on to start the reaction. Aliquots of 1.00 mL were collected in 1 h intervals. 6.00 mL of an acetate buffer solution (0.10 mol L−1) were added to the aliquots to control the pH. Finally, the aliquots were filtered using cellulose membranes (0.45 µm pore size) and analyzed by UV-Vis Molecular Absorption Spectrophotometry (Thermo scientific - Evolution Array).

The degradation efficiency of the MO was calculated using the Eq. (2).

Photodegradation assays were performed under the same conditions described above in the presence of the main species responsible for the dye degradation. The effects of t-butyl alcohol (TBA), AgNO3, Na2EDTA, and p-benzoquinone (p-BZN) which act as inhibitors of, respectively, hydroxyl radicals (∙OH), electrons in the conduction band (
e
BC

), and superoxide radical (∙
O

) [20,35] were evaluated at an initial concentration of 10 mmol L−1. These assays were performed in replicates.

The pH, conductivity, redox potential, Zeta Potential, and quantum yield of the GQDs suspensions obtained by HTC of the BSG at the different temperature and residence time conditions determined by the DOE matrix are presented in Table 2.

The pH values were below 3.13, being very acid due to the high concentration of phosphoric acid (0.10 mol L−1), which was employed as an activator in the BSG’s carbonization process. This acid acts in the biomass’s decomposition, decreasing the formation of tar in the carbonization process [6]. H3PO4 contributes to the attachment of oxygen-containing functional groups on biomass walls and in the aromatization process, forming -COOH, -OH, CC, -PO, P-OOH, and aromatic structures [36,37].

To better understand the H+ ions’ contribution to the HTC process, the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation (Eq. (3)) was evaluated. For this, it was considered that the process involved the consumption of protons and, due to the high availability of this ion, its concentration can be estimated by the pH of the system, i.e.,
pH ~ − log
c (
H
+
)
. Thus, the Eq. (4) is obtained.
(4)
pH - p
H

=
k
′

Thus, the system's pH is proportional to the carbonization rate constant (k′) for a constant time. In this way, to evaluate the behavior of the k′ constant according to the absolute temperature (described by the Arrhenius Equation), the pH of each treatment was used as a function of the reciprocal of the absolute temperature (Fig. S1). The observed linear behavior indicates that a pseudo-first-order mechanism describes all the HTC treatments, as assumed by Eq. (4). Furthermore, the different HTC treatment times did not significantly affect the proton consumption but rather the system temperature. This participation of the H+ in HTC reactions occurs from the ionization of water and organic acids contained in the system, and the consumption of H+ accelerates the carbonization process and reduces energy consumption [38].

The high electrical conductivity values (1–3 mS cm−1) observed for all systems (assays 1–9, Table 2) are attributed to the high concentration of H+ ions. These ions have high molar conductivity (349.8 S cm2 mol−1) [39]. The correlation between conductivity and pH of the GQD suspensions determined by the DOE is shown in Fig. S2. There is a linear relation between pH and conductivity, wherein higher conductivity values can be observed in the more acidic suspensions. The correlation between the redox potential measured at 25 °C and the HTC treatment temperature is shown in Fig. S3. The negative correlation between temperature and redox potential may suggest the formation of more reduced species with the temperature increase, which is compatible with the formation of carbonaceous materials, such as carbon (zero Nox) and graphene, by the consumption of carbonyls and carboxyls (carbon with Nox equal to + 2 and + 3), for example.

The GQDs presented positive zeta potential values, ranging from 3 to 23 mV, as shown in Table 2. These results indicate the presence of positive charges on the surface of the GQDs in suspension. As the system pH is also low (2–3), the Brönsted acid-base groups present on the GQDs, such as carboxyl groups, are protonated and mainly neutral. Among the protonated groups with a positive charge, amino groups can be mentioned, which may be present in smaller quantities at this pH range in the system. It is noteworthy that a positively charged surface layer favors the interaction with negatively charged materials, such as the MO model molecule, which is an anionic dye.

The images obtained by TEM are presented in Fig. 1a–c. Particles of different sizes can be observed as the tendency of the GQDs to form agglomerates [5,40]. Hexagonal rings and zigzag edge structures (Fig. 1c) are commonly observed in GQDs [41,42]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the material obtained from the BSG via HTC process is characterized as being GQDs.

Wang et al. [43] synthesized GQDs via HTC of coffee grounds biomass obtaining graphene layers with 0.22 nm lattice spacing attributed to the graphene plane (100). Chen et al. [44] synthesized GQDs containing graphene layers with 0.204 nm lattice spacing attributed to the graphene plane (101). Other studies have used rice husk biomass, pineapple leaf fiber, and lignin biomass as precursors to GQDs [45–47].

Infrared spectra were obtained for each GQDs sample of the DOE ( Fig. 2). Functional groups typically present in carbon dots can be observed in the samples, such as: the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups (νO-H) at 3250 cm−1; and the symmetrical and symmetrical stretching of Csp3 H bonds (νC-H) at 2900 and 2850 cm−1, attributed to the alkane bonds or the alkyl group [48]. It can also be observed the stretching vibration of carbonyl (νCO) at 1630 cm−1, stretching vibration of amine groups (νC-N) at 1440 cm−1, stretching vibration of νC-O (1030 cm−1) and deformation of C-H bond from aromatic rings at 510 cm−1 [21,31,48,49].

According to El Korhani et al. [31], hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups from the carbohydrates fraction of the BSGs contribute to CDs formation during the HTC. It can be observed that the intensity of the band at 3250 cm−1 decreases with the increase in the temperature and reaction time of the HTC process. These bands may be associated with the presence of carboxylic acids [50,51]. These results corroborate physicochemical results, in which lower pH values and higher conductance values were obtained. This occurs due to the presence of dissociated species in the medium and the more positive redox potential.

The band related to the CN bond (1430 cm−1) is more pronounced in the samples obtained at higher temperatures. This result suggests that the GQDs are self-doped with nitrogen and that the increase in temperature favors the breaking of protein structures in the system [24]. A decrease in the intensity of the band at 1010 cm−1, attributed to the CO bond, along with the appearance of an adjacent band at 935 cm−1 was observed. These results indicate the decrease of oxidized groups with the increase of the HTC process temperature, in agreement with the obtained redox potential results.

The GQDs suspensions were also analyzed by UV–vis absorption spectrophotometry. Two peaks are observed in the absorption spectrum, the less intense at 227 nm and the more intense at 281 nm ( Fig. 3). The first is usually attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds, whereas the latter is attributed to the n-π* transition of hybridization with heteroatoms such as CO or C-N [13,52]. The insert in Fig. 3 shows the bright blue color emitted by the sample under UV light (365 nm).

The coloration emitted by the GQDs can be attributed to the wavelength irradiated, the particle size, and the superficial characteristics of the material. Other researchers observed that an increase in the particle size from 1.2 to 70 nm causes a shift from blue to red color [53,54]. The emission of blue color can be attributed to nitrogen doping [55,56]. Regarding the fluorescence properties, the GQDs obtained from the HTC treatment performed at 150 °C for 14 h showed maximum excitation at 327 nm under light emission fixed at a wavelength of 403 nm and maximum emission at 400 nm under light excitation fixed at 324 nm, a range close to that found in other studies [13,52].

The QY was calculated using Eq. (1) and the obtained values are presented in Table 2. The QY values ranged between 0.3 % and 0.6 % for all HTC treatments, except for treatment 5 (175 °C at 14 h) and treatment 7 (200 °C at 14 h), whose values were higher, 4.4 % and 4.9 %, respectively. The discontinuity in the behavior of the QY concerning the temperature suggests that the formation of more conjugated and rigid groups, which favor higher QY, occurs slowly and requires a higher amount of energy.

The QY values indicate the proportion of absorbed photons to emitted photons and tend to increase with increased temperatures and reaction times (Fig. S4). This is because fluorescence intensifies as the carbonization tends towards the end. It is also observed that the emission wavelength shifts towards blue. In addition, lower QY values may be associated with the lower pH of the GQDs suspensions [51].

MO is an anionic dye (pKa 3.45) widely used as a pH indicator since at pH < 3.1 it is red, at values between 3.1 < pH < 4.4 it becomes orange, and at pH > 4.4 it turns yellow [57]. The change of MO color according to the system pH required its adjustment to obtain the UV–vis spectra. This was accomplished by adding an acetate buffer solution to the aliquots withdrawn from the system before the analysis. The UV–vis absorption spectrum and the analytical curve of MO are shown in Fig. S5.

The materials obtained in the DOE experiments (Table 2) were used in the MO photodegradation assays to investigate the influence of the HTC process temperature (T) and residence time (t) on the photocatalytic characteristics of the GQDs. The obtained results are shown in Fig. 4. The GQDs obtained by treatment 2 (125 °C, 24 h) and treatment 4 (150 °C, 14 h) presented the best results, i.e., degradation greater than 75 %. The experiments performed in the dark presented less than 2 % MO removal, suggesting that the degradation process is more important than the adsorption process [20]. This result was confirmed by the Doehlert matrix analysis, wherein a second-order model described in Eq. (5) relates the MO photodegradation percentage at a reaction time equal to 7 h as a function of T and t.
(5) % Photodegradation = − 268.99 + 4.87 − 0.018 T2 − 0.039 t2 + 0.005 Tt

The response surface was obtained by stepwise treatment, as can be seen in the Fig. 5. The maximum MO photodegradation was achieved using GQDs obtained at 130–150 °C. This maximum region appears because of the negative coefficients of temperature and time quadratic interactions described by the model in Eq. (5). Furthermore, no linear variation in the MO photodegradation percentage occurs when the HTC residence time is varied.

The fact that this region of maximum photodegradation occurs at intermediate HTC temperatures may be related to the fluorescence intensity of the samples. The GQDs from the higher temperature treatment presented higher fluorescence intensity. This suggests that many photons were emitted very quickly during the photodegradation reaction, increasing the recombination rate of the electron-hole pair and decreasing its availability for the oxy-reduction reactions. The suspensions from the treatment at lower temperatures presented low fluorescence intensity, indicating little photon emission and slow generation of photogenerated charge pairs, which decreases the photodegradation efficiency. For this reason, the GQDs suspension obtained at an intermediate temperature (150 °C, 14 h) presented better photodegradation efficiency. Therefore, this condition was chosen for the kinetic study and elucidation of the reaction mechanism.
Based on the best result of the GQDs suspension obtained at 150 °C, 14 h a kinetic study was performed, and the results are shown in Fig. 6.

It can be observed the presence of two steps in the pseudo-first-order mechanism of the reaction due to the slope change of the obtained straight lines. Thus, the curve was divided into a first stage ranging from 0 to 120 min (Stage 1), which can be attributed to the adsorption of MO on the GQDs surface, with a kinetic constant value (k) of 1.00 × 10−3 min−1, whose linear fit presented a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.97. The second stage, ranging from 180 to 600 min (Stage 2), attributed to the MO photodegradation, showed a k of 4.70 × 10−3 min−1, with an R2 value of 0.99, similar to that found in other studies of dye photodegradation [20,51,58].

To investigate the species responsible for the MO degradation, studies involving the addition of inhibitors were performed. Therefore, t-butyl alcohol (TBA), AgNO3, Na2EDTA and p-benzoquinone (p-BZN) were used, which act as inhibitors of hydroxyl radicals (∙OH), electrons (
e
BC
−
)
, photogenerated vacancies (
h
BV
+
)
and superoxide radical (∙
O
2
−
), respectively [20,35]. The results are shown in Fig. 7. It can be observed that the largest contribution to reaction is attributed to the reactive ∙O2 - species, due to strong inhibition of the photodegradation in the presence of p-BZN (∼ 84 % of inhibition), followed by the
e
BC
−
, due to 38 % inhibition promoted by silver ions. The presence of the
e
BC
−
, species confirms the semiconducting behavior of the GQDs.

According to the obtained results, it can be concluded that the GQDs obtained from BSGs absorb UV light, generating electron-hole pairs (
e
BC
−
/h+). The surface defects of the GQDs can act as electron scavengers, decreasing the recombination of
e
BC
−
/h+, releasing the electrons for photocatalytic reactions to occur. In this way, the electrons can either act directly in the dye reduction or be captured by the oxygen of functional groups, following an indirect reduction pathway through superoxide radicals (∙O2 -) formation [59]. Similarly, the h+ participates in the oxidation of the hydroxyl groups present in the aqueous solution to form hydroxyl radicals (∙OH) and in the direct degradation of the dye to generate organic radicals [20,21]. The simplified mechanism is described in the scheme in Fig. 8.

Thus, the GQDs proved to be good electron donors since there is a prevalence of reactions wherein electrons and superoxide radicals are formed, indicated by the higher inhibition caused by p-BZN and AgNO3. These results are in accordance with other works in the literature once the GQDs function as electron sites and can be compared to zero valence metals [60,61].
Other works also promoted the photodegradation of MO and, as can be seen from Table 3, the GQDs see shown as good candidates for removing this type of pollutant in an aqueous medium.

Therefore, it can be concluded that it was possible to synthesize graphene quantum dots from brewer’s spent grains by a sustainable route that adheres to green chemistry principles. An experimental design based on Doehlert Matrix enabled the verification of the temperature and residence time influences in the GQDs proprieties, especially in the photocatalytic efficiency, evaluated by the methyl orange photodegradation. The GQDs obtained under milder temperature conditions presented better performance. These nanomaterials showed excellent photocatalytic properties, being able to degrade the methyl orange dye, proving to be good electron donors since the main reactive species were the radical ∙O2 - and the
e
BC
−
. The self-doping of the GQDs with nitrogen was confirmed by the results of FTIR analyses and the blue color emission by the colloidal suspension. The pH, conductivity, and zeta potential analysis allowed the identification of favorable characteristics for the interaction of the material with the methyl orange. This confirms the potential role of green synthesized GQDs as nanocatalysts and the importance of investing in studies that contribute to a zero-waste dynamic, transforming wastes into new materials with added value.
